food_subgroup: Fermented and pickled vegetable salads with legume and seed garnishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese pickled tea leaf salad (lahpet thoke). FOODON_03311737 (salad food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for lahpet thoke. A0CKW (Salads) is the closest defensible parent. Burmese tea leaf salad is a fermented-leaf-based dish not represented in FoodEx2 at the dish level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese tea leaf salad or lahpet thoke as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: fried dried shrimp (absent in no-meat version)
fried broad beans or yellow split peas
fish sauce (may be omitted in vegetarian version)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese tea leaf salad is approximately 200 g, typically presented as a composed plate with pickled tea leaves at the centre surrounded by garnishes including fried egg, sesame seeds, peanuts, fried beans, tomatoes, and lime.

nutrient_source.title: Lahpet Thoke (Pickled Tea Leaf Salad) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients: fermented/pickled tea leaves, fried egg, sesame seeds, peanuts, fried split peas, tomato, lime juice, garlic, chilli
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for lahpet thoke exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each component. Proportions based on published recipe analyses and culinary literature for standard lahpet thoke (vegetarian, with fried egg, no dried shrimp or fish sauce). Approximate ingredient proportions per 100 g dish: pickled tea leaves 25 g, fried egg 20 g, roasted peanuts 10 g, fried split peas/broad beans 10 g, sesame seeds 5 g, tomato 15 g, cabbage 8 g, fried garlic 3 g, lime juice 2 g, chilli 2 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed salads with legumes and seeds — analogous estimate based on GI values for fermented/pickled vegetables (GI ~15–20), legumes/peas (GI ~22–32), egg (GI ~0), peanuts (GI ~14), sesame seeds (GI ~35), and tomato (GI ~15); composite weighted GI estimated at approximately 32
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lahpet thoke in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated as a weighted composite of the glycemic indices of the main carbohydrate-contributing ingredients: fried split peas/broad beans (~32), sesame seeds (~35), tomato (~15), fermented tea leaves (~15 estimated, very low carbohydrate), peanuts (~14). Egg contributes no glycemic load. Composite GI estimated at 32 (low). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 11.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 7.6, rounded to 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fermented/pickled tea leaves and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: The fermented tea leaves and any added fish sauce or soy-based seasoning contribute approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (620 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced seasoning and confirm no added fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by fermented tea leaves, legumes, seeds, and egg
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 7.5 per 200 g serving, tea leaf salad is a low-GI food that produces a modest postprandial glucose response, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame offset by moderate sodium and cholesterol from fried egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The salad provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (approximately 4.5:1) from peanuts, sesame, and vegetable oils, which supports cardiovascular health; however, the fried egg contributes approximately 72 mg cholesterol per 100 g and the sodium content warrants caution for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea leaf salad, known in Burmese as lahpet thoke (လက်ဖက်သုပ်), is Myanmar's most iconic national dish and a cornerstone of Burmese culinary identity. It is made from fermented or pickled young tea leaves (lahpet) that are tossed together with an array of crunchy garnishes including toasted sesame seeds, roasted peanuts, fried split peas or broad beans, fried garlic, fresh tomatoes, green chillies, and lime juice. In this vegetarian version served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant in Singapore, dried shrimp and fish sauce are omitted and a fried egg (kyar-oo-kyaw) is added as the protein component. The dish has a complex, layered flavour profile: the fermented tea leaves deliver a distinctive earthy bitterness and umami depth, balanced by the acidity of lime, the heat of chilli, and the richness of the fried egg and oily seeds. The texture is equally varied, with soft fermented leaves contrasting against crunchy fried legumes and seeds. Nutritionally, the dish is moderately energy-dense at approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with a good protein content (~8 g/100 g) from the egg, peanuts, and legumes, and a favourable fat profile dominated by unsaturated fats from sesame and peanuts. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. Sodium is a clinical consideration at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to the fermentation process. The dish is rich in dietary fibre (~3.2 g/100 g), iron (~2.8 mg/100 g), and calcium (~95 mg/100 g), partly attributable to the tea leaves themselves, which are a known source of minerals and polyphenols. Lahpet thoke is typically served as a starter, side dish, or light meal in Myanmar and in Burmese restaurants globally, including in Singapore's growing Myanmar food scene.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for lahpet thoke exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient list is well-documented in culinary literature and the USDA component data is high quality.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are ingredient-composition estimates; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available in approved databases. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct measurement becomes available in HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. 2. The input food_name contained a Burmese script parenthetical (လက်ဖက်ထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်) indicating 'tea leaf rice mix with fried egg'; the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Tea Leaf Salad (Vegetarian, with Fried Egg)'. 3. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee' which are incorrect for this dish; these tags have been disregarded. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as the dish contains no hydrogenated oils; this is confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 4.8 g from peanut and sesame oil composition per USDA SR Legacy). 6. The dish is listed as 'no meat' by the restaurant; dried shrimp and fish sauce are excluded from the nutrient calculation. Halal status is marked false as the fermentation process and egg sourcing cannot be confirmed halal without restaurant certification. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous composite estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 11.8 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 7.6, reported as 7.5. 8. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated values from USDA FoodData Central component data; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record with all sections.
